one_line_takeaway: Content creators can watermark their text against LLM training via injected 'copyright traps', but only long (100-token) sequences repeated ~1000 times become reliably detectable (AUC 0.748) in a real 1.3B model that otherwise defeats state-of-the-art membership inference.
key_finding: Existing document-level MIAs collapse on this low-memorization model (Meeus 2023b AUC 0.513, Shi 2023 AUC 0.524, vs 0.86/0.88 in their original larger-model settings), confirming they are uninformative for content creators. Injected traps CAN restore detectability, but only when long and heavily repeated: the best setup L_ref=100, n_rep=1000 reaches Ratio-MIA AUC 0.748, while short/medium traps repeated 100 times stay near chance. Detectability rises monotonically with training steps, increases with sequence perplexity (Pearson 0.715, p=0.02; ~0.65 low-perplexity to ~0.8 high-perplexity), and survives aggressive quantization (int4 AUC 0.697). Real (in-distribution) sequences are no easier to detect than synthetic ones (L=50, n_rep=100 Ratio AUC 0.492), disproving the 'outliers memorize more easily' intuition for repetition alone.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'copyright traps' for LLMs: purposefully-designed fictitious 'trap sequences' that a content creator injects (repeated many times) into their own document before publication, so that the document's use in LLM training can later be detected via a black-box membership inference attack (MIA). This targets the case existing document-level MIAs cannot handle - models that do NOT naturally memorize (e.g. medium-size ~1B models trained on trillions of tokens). The authors' distinctive contribution is a randomized controlled experiment: they inject traps into real public-domain books and train a real 1.3B LLM (CroissantLLM) from scratch on 3 trillion tokens with the traps embedded, then measure detectability. This lets them draw causal (not just post-hoc correlational) conclusions about which sequence properties (length, repetition count, perplexity, context) drive memorization. Prior cited work (Meeus 2023b, Shi 2023) relies on naturally-occurring memorization and does not inject traps - the paper explicitly positions itself against these as insufficient for low-memorization models.
threat_model: Attacker = an original content creator holding a document D that may be scraped for LLM training. Assumptions: (1) black-box access to a public reference LM (LLaMA-2 7B here) usable both to generate synthetic trap sequences and to score them; (2) black-box (loss/log-prob) query access to the target LM after training. Before releasing the document, the attacker injects a unique trap sequence M_D of controlled length and perplexity at n_rep random positions, producing D'. Goal: infer whether D' was in the target model's training set. Because M_D is unique to D, a sequence-level MIA on M_D is used as a lower-bound proxy for document-level membership. Note this is a defensive/provenance-detection framing (content creators auditing model developers), not an attack that degrades the model; the paper also distinguishes traps from backdoor attacks (traps do not aim to trigger a target classification).
limitations: (1) Even the best trap barely reaches AUC 0.748 - useful but far from reliable single-document certainty; short/medium traps are essentially undetectable. (2) Effectiveness requires long sequences repeated up to 1000x, which harms readability; the authors concede this is impractical for e.g. book authors and only demonstrate feasibility for web publishers using invisible-but-scrapable HTML. (3) The method is defeatable by sequence-level deduplication (they argue this is currently rare/expensive but it is a direct countermeasure). (4) Only ONE target model (1.3B CroissantLLM) was trained, so generalization across architectures/scales is asserted, not shown. (5) Sequence-level MIA is used as a lower-bound proxy for the actual document-level task. (6) Ethically, publishing the method could help malicious developers evade detection, which the authors acknowledge in their impact statement.

datasets_used: 9,542 public-domain Project Gutenberg books (not overlapping PG-19), each >=5000 tokens, used as trap injection hosts
500 member + 500 non-member clean books for the document-level MIA baseline
CroissantLLM training corpus: 3 trillion tokens / 4.1TB unique English-French-Code data
The Pile (uncopyrighted version) used to analyze perplexity of naturally-occurring duplicates as a confounder
quantitative_results: Existing document-level MIA on non-trap books: Meeus 2023b AUC 0.513 +/- 0.021; Shi 2023 (Min-K% Prob) AUC 0.524 +/- 0.003 (vs 0.86 / 0.88 in their original settings)
Best trap setup L_ref=100, n_rep=1000: Ratio MIA AUC 0.748 (Loss 0.611, Min-K% 0.599)
L_ref=100, n_rep=100: Ratio AUC 0.639; L_ref=50, n_rep=1000: Ratio AUC 0.627
Short/medium traps near chance: L_ref=25 even at n_rep=1000 max AUC ~0.557; L_ref=50, n_rep=100 Ratio AUC 0.521
Real (in-distribution) trap sequences L_ref=50, n_rep=100: Ratio AUC 0.492 (disproves outlier hypothesis)
Perplexity vs detectability (L=100, n_rep=1000): Pearson r=0.715, p=0.02; AUC ~0.65 low-perplexity to ~0.8 high-perplexity
Context-augmented Ratio MIA (100-token context) L=50 n_rep=1000: 0.627 -> 0.685; L=25 n_rep=1000: 0.557 -> 0.603
Quantization robustness (L=100, n_rep=1000, Ratio): float32 0.748, float16 0.745, int8 0.738, int4 0.697
Trap-containing documents were <0.04% of tokens seen during training; each trap seen twice
Target model: 1.3B params, 24 layers, hidden 2048, trained 17 days on 30 nodes x 8 A100 GPUs
baselines_compared: Meeus et al. 2023b document-level MIA (meta-classifier over histogram features)
Shi et al. 2023 Min-K% Prob document-level MIA (k=20)
Loss attack (Yeom et al. 2018) - sequence-level MIA
Ratio attack (Carlini et al. 2021, reference = LLaMA-2 7B) - sequence-level MIA (best performer)
